title: Particulate and Electromagnetic Radiation
narrationScript: When an unstable nucleus rearranges itself, the energy it sheds leaves as radiation, and that radiation comes in two fundamentally different families. One family is particles, actual pieces of matter flung out at high speed. The other is electromagnetic waves, pure energy with no mass at all. A radiographer needs both families straight, because one of them makes your image and the other ones shape your safety practice. Start with the particles. The alpha particle is the heavyweight, two protons and two neutrons bound together, identical to a helium nucleus. It carries a double positive charge, and that charge is its undoing. An alpha particle barrels through matter ripping electrons off every atom it passes, dumping its energy in a very short distance. A sheet of paper stops it. A couple of inches of air stops it. The dead outer layer of your own skin stops it. So alpha is no external hazard at all, but get an alpha emitter inside the body, inhaled or swallowed, and all that ionizing power unloads directly into living tissue. Alpha emitters appear around radiography mostly in older radium contamination stories and in licensing exam questions, but the lesson holds. Particle radiation hazards depend on where the emitter is. Next, the beta particle. A beta is an electron ejected from the nucleus itself, born at the moment a neutron converts to a proton. It carries a single negative charge and almost no mass, about one seven-thousandth of an alpha. Lighter and faster, it penetrates farther, typically a few feet of air or a few millimeters into skin and aluminum foil territory. A modest thickness of aluminum or plastic stops it cold. Betas matter to us for two reasons. First, many gamma sources, including iridium one ninety two and cobalt sixty, emit betas along with their gammas. The source capsule wall absorbs them, which is one of the jobs of the capsule. Second, beta emitters on the skin or inside the body are a genuine hazard, so contamination control around any leaking source is serious business. Third, the neutron. Neutrons are the odd particle, as heavy as a proton but with no charge at all. No charge means nothing pushes back, so neutrons sail through the electron clouds and dense, heavy shielding that stop everything else. Lead does surprisingly little. What slows a neutron is collision with something its own size, a hydrogen nucleus, which is why neutron shielding is hydrogen rich material, water, polyethylene, concrete, paraffin. Neutron radiography is a specialized method all its own, good at imaging light, hydrogen bearing materials inside metal assemblies, exactly the reverse of what X rays do well. For everyday gamma radiography, you meet neutrons only at the reactor where sources are made. Now the second family, electromagnetic radiation. X rays and gamma rays are not particles. They are photons, bundles of pure electromagnetic energy traveling at the speed of light, with no mass and no charge. Here is the point that surprises people. An X ray photon and a gamma ray photon of the same energy are physically identical. Nothing about the photon itself tells you which it is. The only difference is birthplace. Gamma rays come from inside an unstable nucleus as it settles toward stability. X rays are made outside the nucleus, in the electron shells or in machines, when fast electrons slam into a target and decelerate. Because photons carry no charge and no mass, they interact with matter only by chance encounters, which makes them deeply penetrating, able to pass through inches of steel. There is no thickness that stops every photon. Shielding thins a photon beam exponentially, layer by layer, each half value layer cutting the field in half, but never to zero. That penetrating power is exactly why photons, not particles, are the working radiation of radiography. A beam that can cross the full thickness of a weld and still register on film can carry information about voids, inclusions, and cracks hidden inside. The particles cannot do that job from outside the part. So file the family portrait. Alpha, heavy, double charge, stopped by paper, an internal hazard only. Beta, light, single charge, stopped by a little aluminum, a skin and contamination concern, absorbed by the source capsule. Neutron, neutral, stopped by hydrogen rich material, the tool of a specialized cousin method. And the photons, X ray and gamma, massless, chargeless, identical twins separated only by origin, penetrating, and the reason this method exists. Next lesson we look closely at how each twin is produced and what energies our sources and machines actually deliver.
imagePrompt: A radiographer stands at a job-site table with a gamma projector and survey meter, while behind him a yellow-and-magenta radiation boundary rope and warning signs cordon off a large steel pipe spool awaiting exposure.

sections.heading: Two Families of Radiation
sections.body:
Radiation emitted by unstable nuclei - and by the machines we build - divides into two fundamentally different families:

- **Particulate radiation**: actual pieces of matter ejected at high speed - **alpha particles, beta particles, and neutrons**. They have mass; alphas and betas also carry charge.
- **Electromagnetic radiation**: **X-rays and gamma rays** - photons of pure energy with **no mass and no charge**, traveling at the speed of light.

The distinction is practical, not academic. Charged particles interact continuously with the electrons of any material they enter, so they dump their energy over a short, definite range - easily stopped, but intensely ionizing along the way. Photons interact only by chance encounters, so they penetrate deeply and are attenuated gradually, never stopped absolutely. That penetrating behavior is why photons are the working radiation of radiography, while the particles mostly shape source design, contamination control, and safety thinking.

sections.heading: Alpha Particles: Heavy, Charged, Short-Ranged
sections.body:
The **alpha particle** is two protons plus two neutrons - a helium nucleus - ejected mostly by very heavy nuclides such as radium and uranium. It is the heavyweight of radiation: roughly 7,300 times the mass of an electron, carrying a **+2 charge**.

That double charge makes alpha a furious ionizer and a feeble traveler. Tearing electrons off every atom it passes, an alpha spends its energy within **an inch or two of air**, and is stopped by **a sheet of paper or the dead outer layer of skin**. Externally, alpha radiation is essentially no hazard.

The danger reverses if an alpha emitter gets **inside the body** - inhaled, swallowed, or through a wound. Then every decay unloads its full ionizing punch directly into living tissue with no dead layer to absorb it. Industrial radiographers rarely meet alpha emitters on the job today, but the principle - **hazard depends on where the emitter is** - underlies all contamination control, including the leak tests required on every sealed source.

sections.heading: Beta Particles: Light, Fast, and Capsule-Stopped
sections.body:
A **beta particle** is an **electron ejected from the nucleus**, created at the instant a neutron converts into a proton. (That conversion is exactly how neutron-rich activation products move back toward the band of stability.) A beta carries a **−1 charge** and about **1/7,300 of an alpha's mass**, so it travels faster and farther: typically **several feet in air**, a few millimeters into tissue, stopped by **a thin sheet of aluminum or plastic**.

Two facts matter to a radiographer:

- **Your gamma sources are beta emitters too.** Ir-192 and Co-60 each emit a beta before their gamma rays. The stainless-steel **source capsule absorbs the betas**, which is one reason sources are sealed in capsules rather than used bare.
- **Contamination is the beta hazard.** A beta emitter resting on skin or inside the body irradiates tissue at point-blank range. This is why a suspected leaking source - a failed capsule - turns a routine job into a regulated contamination event immediately.

sections.heading: Neutrons: No Charge, Different Rules
sections.body:
The **neutron** is as heavy as a proton but **electrically neutral**, and that changes everything. With no charge, a neutron ignores electron clouds entirely; it interacts only when it strikes a nucleus. High-Z shielding that defeats photons does little - **lead is a poor neutron shield**.

What slows a neutron is a collision partner of similar mass: the **hydrogen nucleus**. Like a billiard ball hitting another ball its own size, a neutron hands off the most energy to hydrogen. Effective neutron shielding is therefore **hydrogen-rich**: water, polyethylene, paraffin, concrete.

For gamma and X-ray radiographers, free neutrons appear mainly at the reactor where sources are activated. But neutrons also support a specialized cousin method, **neutron radiography**, which images in reverse of X-rays: neutrons stream through heavy metals yet are strongly attenuated by hydrogen-bearing materials, making them ideal for finding rubber seals, explosives, adhesives, or corrosion products hidden inside metal assemblies.

sections.caption: Penetrating power compared: paper stops alpha, thin aluminum stops beta, lead thins gamma exponentially, and hydrogen-rich material slows neutrons.

sections.heading: X-rays and Gamma Rays: Identical Twins, Different Birthplaces
sections.body:
**X-rays and gamma rays are photons** - electromagnetic energy in transit, the same physical stuff as light and radio waves but at enormously higher energy and shorter wavelength. A photon has **no mass and no charge**; it travels at the speed of light and interacts with matter only through chance encounters with atoms.

Here is the fact that settles a hundred exam questions: **a 0.5 MeV X-ray photon and a 0.5 MeV gamma photon are physically identical**. Nothing about the photon reveals which it is. The names record only the **origin**:

- **Gamma rays** are born **inside the nucleus**, emitted as an unstable nucleus sheds excess energy after decay.
- **X-rays** are born **outside the nucleus** - from electron interactions, as when machine-accelerated electrons slam into a tungsten target.

Because photons are chargeless, matter thins a photon beam **exponentially** - each half-value layer cuts intensity in half, but no thickness stops every photon. That deep, information-carrying penetration is why photons, not particles, make radiographs: a beam that crosses two inches of weld and still exposes the film carries a shadow-picture of everything it passed through.

sections.caption: Properties of the radiation types a radiographer must know.
rows: Internal hazard only; drives contamination control
Emitted by Ir-192/Co-60; absorbed by the source capsule

sections.caption: X-rays and gamma rays occupy the short-wavelength, high-energy end of the electromagnetic spectrum - identical photons distinguished only by where they are born.

sections.title: Penetrating power is the hazard you cannot see
sections.body: Alpha and beta announce themselves only on contamination instruments, but **photons reach you from across the work area** - through air, plywood, even the far side of a pipe rack. No thickness of shielding stops every photon; shielding only thins the field. That is why gamma radiography runs on **surveyed boundaries, time, distance, and shielding together** - and why you survey after **every** exposure instead of trusting the crank. The Radiation Safety course covers the full discipline; never start it casually.

figures.title: Penetrating power of alpha, beta, gamma, and neutron radiation
X-rays and gamma rays on the electromagnetic spectrum

checkQuestions.question: An alpha particle consists of:
options: Two protons and two neutrons - a helium nucleus
A single high-speed electron
A neutral particle the mass of a proton
A photon of nuclear origin
checkQuestions.explanation: Alpha is the heavy particle: 2 protons + 2 neutrons with a +2 charge. Its double charge makes it intensely ionizing but so short-ranged that paper or dead skin stops it.

checkQuestions.question: Why is an alpha emitter dangerous mainly when taken inside the body?
options: Inside the body it converts to gamma radiation
Externally its energy is absorbed by the dead skin layer, but internally every decay dumps intense ionization directly into living tissue
Body fluids accelerate alpha particles to higher energy
It is not - alpha is harmless everywhere
checkQuestions.explanation: Alpha cannot penetrate the dead outer skin layer, so external exposure is trivial. Inhaled or ingested, the same particles deliver their full ionizing energy point-blank to live tissue.

checkQuestions.question: Lead is a poor shield against neutrons because:
options: Neutrons are repelled by lead's positive nuclei
Neutrons have no charge and pass electron clouds; they are slowed best by collisions with similar-mass hydrogen nuclei in water, polyethylene, or concrete
Lead becomes radioactive and re-emits the neutrons
Neutrons travel too slowly for lead to affect them
checkQuestions.explanation: With no charge, a neutron interacts only by striking nuclei, and it transfers energy most efficiently to a nucleus its own size - hydrogen. Hydrogen-rich materials are the effective neutron shields.

checkQuestions.question: The ONLY difference between an X-ray photon and a gamma-ray photon of equal energy is:
options: The gamma photon carries charge and the X-ray does not
The gamma photon has mass and the X-ray does not
Their origin - gamma rays come from the nucleus, X-rays from electron interactions outside it
Gamma photons travel faster than X-ray photons
checkQuestions.explanation: Both are massless, chargeless photons traveling at light speed; at equal energy they are physically identical. The names record birthplace only: nucleus for gamma, electron processes (such as machine targets) for X-rays.

checkQuestions.question: Which property of X-rays and gamma rays makes them - rather than alpha or beta particles - the working radiation of radiography?
options: They ionize matter more intensely per millimeter than alpha particles
Their deep, exponential penetration lets them cross the full part thickness and carry an image to the film
They can be deflected by magnets to scan the part
They are completely stopped by the first half-value layer
checkQuestions.explanation: Charged particles spend their energy in a short range and cannot cross thick metal. Chargeless photons penetrate deeply and attenuate gradually, so the transmitted beam still carries internal-condition information to the detector.

references: CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Basic Radiographic Physics 4.1 (particulate radiation - alpha, beta, neutron)
CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Basic Radiographic Physics 4.2 (electromagnetic radiation - X-ray, gamma ray)
Shielding behavior of photons (HVL concept) treated quantitatively in module rt1-3 (reference forward)
